summary: I completed my Mechanical Engineering degree at the Illinois Institute of Technology before entering the service in 1944. It was a solid foundation that prepared me for my work at Bendix Aviation and later for the Army Specialized Training Program. Having that degree definitely shaped my military and post-military experiences.

After finishing my degree at Illinois Institute of Technology, I worked at Bendix Aviation in South Bend, Indiana, specifically in the carburetor division. I was about 21 when I started there and turned 22 the year I was drafted into the Army. It was an interesting place, and I even heard some stories about other soldiers who worked there after the war.

I was drafted into the United States Army in August 1944, despite initially wanting to join the Navy. After induction, I went through basic training at Camp Fannin, Texas, and later participated in the Army Specialized Training Program at Ohio State University. My time in the Army involved various roles, including photo topography training with the 29th Engineers.

Right after I was inducted into the Army, I was sent to Fort Sheridan where we got our haircuts and GI clothing issued. It was a brief stop before being put on a train and sent down to Camp Fannin, Texas for basic training. Fort Sheridan was essentially the first step in my Army journey.

Camp Fannin, located between Tyler and Kilgore, Texas, was where I completed my basic infantry training. I spent about 13 to 16 weeks there, during which I applied for Officers' Candidate School and the Army Specialized Training Program. The experience was tough but memorable, and I met a lot of interesting people during that time.

Tyler, Texas was near Camp Fannin where I did my basic training. Although I didn't spend much time in Tyler itself, it was part of the region that shaped my early military experience. Being in East Texas was quite a change from Chicago.

Kilgore, Texas was another town close to Camp Fannin where I underwent basic training. Like Tyler, it was part of the East Texas area that I became familiar with during my early Army days. The whole region was new to me coming from Chicago.

While I was at Camp Fannin, I applied for Officers' Candidate School, hoping to advance my military career. However, the Army prioritized my orders for the Army Specialized Training Program instead, so I didn't attend OCS at that time. It was interesting to see how the Army made those decisions.

I was assigned to the Army Specialized Training Program at Ohio State University after basic training. There, we took refresher engineering courses from December 1944 to about February 1945, living in bunks under the football stadium seats. It was a much better experience than basic, with good food and a lot of free time to explore Columbus.

Before joining the Army, I spent several summers working on a farm in Iowa with a family who were friends. Those experiences gave me a taste of rural life and were my first times away from home for extended periods. It was a meaningful part of my youth before the war.

I attended Tilden High School where I was part of the ROTC program for three years under Sergeant Moore. That early military training gave me some familiarity with military life before I was drafted. It was a good foundation for what came later in the Army.

Ohio State University was where I attended the Army Specialized Training Program starting around December 1944. We lived in the football stadium and took refresher engineering courses for a couple of months. It was a unique military experience, quite different from basic training, and I got to know Columbus through friends there.

Columbus, Ohio was the city where Ohio State University is located, and where I spent several months in the Army Specialized Training Program. I had some free time to explore the city with a friend from Zanesville, which helped me get around and enjoy the area. It was quite a change from Texas and Chicago.

After completing ASTP, I was selected as one of seventy-eight people to join the 29th Engineer Combat Battalion in Portland, Oregon. We trained in photo topography for about three months before some of us, including myself, were reassigned to Fort Lewis. It was a specialized unit focusing on mapping and engineering tasks.

After training with the 29th Engineers in Portland, I was among thirty-nine soldiers sent to Fort Lewis, Washington. This reassignment came after the Army reduced the number of personnel needed for the photo topography unit. Fort Lewis was part of my later Army service.

Portland, Oregon was where I trained with the 29th Engineer Combat Battalion in photo topography. We were housed at Holman School, a former elementary school, and received instruction from military instructors. It was a fascinating experience learning how to create maps using aerial photographs and 3D visualization techniques.

Holman School in Portland, Oregon, was the site of our photo topography training with the 29th Engineers. It had been an elementary school before the Army used it for military instruction. We learned from military instructors how to interpret aerial photos and create detailed maps.

Camp Stoneman was not mentioned in detail in my story, so I don't have personal experiences to share about it. It may have been part of the broader military network during my service, but it wasn't a place I was stationed at or trained in.

San Francisco was not directly part of my military narrative in this interview. While it was a major military hub during the war, my training and assignments took me to places like Camp Fannin, Ohio State, Portland, and Fort Lewis instead.

Manila was not mentioned in my military story, so I don't have any personal connections or experiences related to that place during my service. My Army journey was focused more on training and assignments within the United States.
